instruction: Explain the difference between a pointer and a reference.

input.title: Pointers vs References

output.technical: A pointer (`int*`) holds a memory address, can be reassigned, and can be null. A reference (`int&`) is an alias for an existing object, cannot be null, and cannot be reseated after initialization.
output.beginner: A pointer is like an address written on a piece of paper (which can be blank). A reference is just a nickname for a person standing right next to you.
output.analogy: A pointer is a URL link to a webpage. A reference is simply opening the webpage in a new tab.
output.solution: Use references by default for function parameters, and use pointers only when the value can optionally be null or when managing dynamic memory.
output.prevention: Avoid using raw pointers when references will suffice. Always check pointers for null before dereferencing.

instruction: Explain the difference between a pointer and a reference.

input.title: Pointers vs References

output.technical: A pointer (`int*`) holds a memory address, can be reassigned, and can be null. A reference (`int&`) is an alias for an existing object, cannot be null, and cannot be reseated after initialization.
output.beginner: A pointer is like an address written on a piece of paper (which can be blank). A reference is just a nickname for a person standing right next to you.
output.analogy: A pointer is a URL link to a webpage. A reference is simply opening the webpage in a new tab.
output.solution: Use references by default for function parameters, and use pointers only when the value can optionally be null or when managing dynamic memory.
output.prevention: Avoid using raw pointers when references will suffice. Always check pointers for null before dereferencing.
